mnemonics.title: GPU Memory Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: F-R-O-S-T: Fastest Registers → On-chip Shared memory → Texture/Constant cache → Global memory (slowest)

mnemonics.title: CPU vs GPU Design
mnemonics.content: CPU: Complex Control, Big Cache, Few Cores (CCC) vs GPU: Simple Control, Small Cache, Thousands of Cores (CST)

acronyms.usage: Vector operations on arrays like adding two vectors element-wise
GPU execution model where threads run same instruction independently

acronyms.expansion: General-Purpose computing on Graphics Processing Units
acronyms.usage: Using GPU for non-graphics computations like machine learning
Basic GPU compute unit containing multiple cores
Parallelism from running multiple threads simultaneously
Parallelism from overlapping instructions within a single thread

visualTips.visualization: Imagine 32 people (threads) doing the same exercise (instruction) in a gym - they move together. If one needs different exercise (branch), others wait or do different thing separately.
Single core = one person doing all tasks one by one. Multicore = multiple people each doing different tasks simultaneously.
Think of a restaurant with many waiters (warps): when one is waiting for kitchen (memory), others take orders - no waiting!
Shared memory = your team's whiteboard (fast, small, shared). Global memory = library across town (slow, huge, everyone uses).

analogies.analogy: GPU is like a factory assembly line with thousands of workers doing simple repetitive tasks; CPU is like a skilled craftsman doing complex tasks quickly.
Thread block is like a team of workers assigned to one workstation - they can share tools (shared memory) and coordinate (synchronize).

analogies.analization: Occupancy is like factory utilization - more workers active means better efficiency, but you need enough work to keep everyone busy.

analogies.analogy: Branch divergence in a warp is like a dance troupe where most dancers continue routine but one needs to do something different - they must wait or split.
